perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Inform ation” means any and all confidential and/or proprietary information disclosed by the Casino t o the Receiving Party, which may in clude without limitation: financial information, business information, m arketing d ata, trade secrets, and other data, materials, products, plans, specifications, reports, manuals, computer software or programs, cont ractual relationships and other similar information delivered in any form or media.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means any and all information disclosed by the Casino to the Receiving Party, including: financial information, business information, marketing data, and other data, materials, products, plans, specifications, reports, manuals, computer software or programs, contractual relationships and other similar information delivered in any form or media. Trade secrets are explicitly excluded from this definition.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract contradicts established trade secret laws that protect such information. This exclusion undermines the purpose of the NDA and could render it unenforceable regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute § 134.90 protects trade secrets, defining them broadly. By excluding trade secrets from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract attempts to remove this category of information from protection, conflicting directly with the statute's intent and scope.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90 (2022) — Uniform trade secrets act. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 134 - Miscellaneous trade regulations. 134.90 - Uniform trade secrets act. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 134.90 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 134.90 Uniform trade secrets act. (1) Definitions. In this section: (a) “Improper means" includes espionage, theft, bribery, misrepresentation and breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy. (b) “Readily ascertainable" information does not include information accessible through a license agreement or by an employee under a confidentiality agreement with his or her employer. (c) “Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process to which all of the following apply: 1. The information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. 2. The information is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy that are reasonable under the circumstances. (2) Misappropriation. No person, including the state, may misappropriate or threaten to misappropriate a trade secret by doing any of the following: (a) Acquiring the trade secret of another by means which the person knows or has reason to know constitute improper means. (b) Disclosing or using without express or implied consent a trade secret of another if the person did any of the following: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes trade secrets from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' while Wisconsin Statute § 134.90 protects trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Wisconsin Statutes § 134.90, defines 'trade secret' and outlines protections against misappropriation. By excluding trade secrets, the modified contract attempts to circumvent these protections, creating a direct conflict with the law. The contract's altered definition weakens the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under Wisconsin law, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Receiving Party s hall protect the Confidentia l Information with at leas t t he same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no case, less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Receiving Party shall protect the Confidential Information with the same degree of care that it uses to protect publicly available information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the standard of care for protecting confidential information to the level used for publicly available information, which is essentially no protection. This contradicts the reasonable care standard often required to protect confidential data, as it essentially permits the receiving party to handle confidential information with a level of indifference, rendering the confidentiality clause largely meaningless.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Duty of Care under Wisconsin Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Wisconsin law, the general duty of care requires individuals and entities to act with reasonable care to avoid causing harm to others. This change directly conflicts with that duty by explicitly requiring confidential information to only be protected at the same level of protection as public information, which can be none. This creates a standard of care so low as to be negligent by definition, violating that duty of care.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the standard of protecting confidential information with the same degree of care used for "publicly available information." This creates a clear legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this essentially provides no protection. The Law Explanation supports this, stating the revised standard is so low it constitutes negligence, violating the general duty of care. While no law snippets were provided, the logical conclusion, based on the explanation given, is that the updated text directly conflicts with the expectation of reasonable care, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Receiving Party shall have no obligations unde r this Agreement with respect to Conf idential Information if it can demonstrate tha t the information: ( a) is publicly av ailable at the time of disclosure; (b) be comes publ icly a vailable after di sclosure through no act of the receiving pa rty or its repr esentatives; (c) is in the receiving party’s possession free of obligation; (d)is deve loped by the receiving party without use of the Confidential Information or through other breach of this Agreement; ( e ) is disclosed to the receiving party by a third party without obligation; (e) is not identifiable a s confidential; o r ( f) is required to be publ icly disclosed pursuant to a properly executed subpoena or other regulatory or court order (“Order”), provided the receiving party ( i ) gives r easonable w ritten not ice to the disclosing pa rty, ( ii) gives the disclosing party a reasonable oppor tunity to respond to the Order, and (iii) limits dis closure to tha t portion of the Confidential Information re quired b y the Order.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Receiving Party shall have no obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information if it can demonstrate that the information: (a) is publicly available at the time of disclosure; (b) becomes publicly available after disclosure through no act of the receiving party or its representatives; (c) is in the receiving party’s possession free of obligation; (d) is developed by the receiving party without use of the Confidential Information or through other breach of this Agreement; (e) is disclosed to the receiving party by a third party without obligation; (e) is not identifiable as confidential; or (f) is required to be publicly disclosed pursuant to a properly executed subpoena or other regulatory or court order (“Order”).
perturbation.explanation: Removing the conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) from section 5(f) removes the Receiving Party's obligation to provide notice to the Casino before disclosing confidential information under a subpoena or court order. This directly contradicts legal standards related to protecting privileged or confidential information during legal proceedings, potentially waiving protections that would otherwise be afforded to trade secrets or proprietary business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(3)(A) provides protections for individuals subject to subpoenas, including the right to object and require the serving party to obtain a court order to compel production of protected information. By removing the receiving party's obligation to notify the disclosing party (Casino) before complying with a subpoena, the modified contract prevents the disclosing party from exercising their rights under FRCP 45(d)(3)(A) to protect confidential information from disclosure in legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the receiving party's obligation to notify the disclosing party before complying with a subpoena, which directly contradicts the protections afforded to individuals subject to subpoenas. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the mechanics of issuing a subpoena (e.g., 'Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued'), the Law Explanation suggests that FRCP 45(d)(3)(A) provides a right to object and protect confidential information. By removing the notification requirement, the changed text prevents the disclosing party from exercising their rights to protect confidential information, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. T his A greement shall ha ve a term of three (3) years. Receiving P arty’s obligations in relation to technical and personally- identifiable inf ormation shall be inde finite.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall have a term of three (3) years. Receiving Party’s obligations shall terminate after three years.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the indefinite term for technical and personally identifiable information, the modified contract could allow for the disclosure of trade secrets or other confidential technical information after three years, even if those trade secrets remain valuable and protected under law. This conflicts with the potentially perpetual protection afforded to properly maintained trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wisconsin Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute § 134.90(2) prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. Trade secrets can be protected indefinitely as long as the owner takes reasonable measures to maintain their secrecy and the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified contract's three-year limit on obligations could allow the Receiving Party to misappropriate trade secrets after that period, directly contradicting the statute's perpetual protection for valid trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract contradicts Wisconsin Statute § 134.90(2) regarding trade secret protection. The original contract provided indefinite protection for technical and personally identifiable information, while the changed contract limits this protection to three years. The Law Explanation states that the statute prohibits misappropriation of trade secrets, which can be protected indefinitely if secrecy is maintained and the information has economic value. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly reference this statute, the Law Explanation explicitly points to the contradiction, stating the three-year limit could allow misappropriation after that period, contradicting the statute's perpetual protection for valid trade secrets. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation.
